title: Ginger compress for muscle ache
subtitle: A warming topical compress to ease localised muscle pain
excerpt: A strong ginger root infusion applied as a hot compress to a specific area of muscle ache or mild joint pain. The ginger drives heat deep into the tissues, stimulates local circulation, and eases stiffness. A classic Japanese and western herbal first-response for localised muscular soreness.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), ginger entry. Ginger as a warming counter-irritant for muscular and rheumatic complaints. Long-standing use in Japanese traditional medicine (the ginger compress, shoukankyu) and in western herbal practice for localised muscular and rheumatic pain.

content.text: A hot ginger brew soaked into a cloth and held against a stiff, aching muscle or a mildly sore joint. The ginger compress is a long-standing form in Japanese traditional medicine, where it is known as shoukankyu, and is used at home as well as by practitioners. In the western tradition, ginger has long been used as a

preparation for muscular and joint complaints. The compress works through direct heat, ginger drawing blood to the surface, and the way the gingerols dull local pain. The trick is to apply it as hot as is comfortable, re-soak the cloth as it cools, and keep the area covered and warm between applications.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Skin sensitivity: ginger is a rubefacient, it causes redness and warmth. This is the intended effect. However, some people develop an uncomfortable burning sensation, blistering, or hives at higher concentrations. Test on a small area of skin first. The compress should feel warming, not burning.

Do not apply to inflamed, hot-to-touch joints, for acute hot inflammation (gout attack, acute arthritis flare), heat is contraindicated. Cold is the appropriate application for hot, acutely inflamed joints; warming preparations like ginger are for stiff, cold, aching muscles and chronic rather than acute joint pain.

Do not apply to broken skin, rashes, or skin conditions.

See your doctor for joint or muscle pain that is severe, unexplained, accompanied by fever, or that does not respond to rest and self-care within a week.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

content.text: Slice {{ginger-root}} of fresh ginger thinly and place in the small saucepan with {{water}} of water. Simmer covered for 15 minutes. Strain through a sieve into a wide bowl or jug. Keep the liquid as hot as possible, the compress needs to be hot.

Fold the muslin cloth or a clean flannel into a pad. Dip it into the hot ginger liquid, squeeze out the excess carefully (the liquid is hot, squeeze with gloves or through a dry cloth). Apply the soaked compress to the aching muscle or joint as hot as is comfortably tolerable. Hold in place for three to five minutes, then re-soak and repeat. Continue for 15 to 20 minutes, keeping the compress hot by re-soaking frequently.

One compress session of 15 to 20 minutes, once or twice daily. The warming and analgesic effect typically peaks during the application and continues for 30 to 60 minutes afterwards. For best results, keep the area warm after the compress, wrap in a flannel or dry cloth.
When NOT to use this remedy

Do not apply to acutely inflamed, hot, swollen joints. Do not apply to broken or irritated skin. See your doctor for unexplained joint swelling, joint pain with fever, muscle pain with weakness or neurological symptoms, or pain following injury.
